new_findings.topic: Reddit LTV vs. other traffic sources
new_findings.title: Reddit organic NSFW traffic produces the highest fan LTV of any channel

variants.claim: Fans acquired through niche-matched NSFW Reddit posts have significantly higher LTV than Instagram or dating-app traffic because they self-selected into a subreddit specifically for their fetish and actively chose to view the content, indicating high purchase intent.
variants.what_they_do: Agency benchmarks LTV by traffic source. Reddit organic NSFW: $8–$35 LTV on free pages. Dating app traffic also historically high LTV. Instagram traffic lower. Agency prioritises Reddit despite declining ROI trend because subscriber quality (intentionality) remains superior. At peak (3 years ago) one viral SFW post could drive 3,000–5,000 subs/day; currently realistic target is 10+ paid subs/day per account on a well-matched creator.

variants.claim: Reddit traffic must be run manually with niche-matched content and captions per subreddit because, unlike IG/X, the poster is effectively the algorithm choosing the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media keeps 100 manual Reddit VAs (each issued a physical phone within four weeks) matching niche content and captioning to specific subreddits while fully automating IG/X/Threads; Reddit free subs cost $0.25-$1 and return $4-$8.

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

variants.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.

sources.quote: we do see a way way higher LTV from these fans off of Reddit, off of the regular Reddit strategy than we see from Instagram fans
I think it's safe to say that that's the best way to get the highest um highest quality of subscriber to the account
On Reddit, you are the algorithm essentially. You are determining which subreddits
cost per acquisition of a Reddit subscriber is anywhere from 25 cents to a dollar... getting anywhere from $4 to $8 a return
I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit
Twitter's the best yeah Twitter okay so Twitter's the best okay um Reddit though would weigh when we start getting that going so I know that's very good for traffic
that's like I would say that's like in my opinion like a super outdated method like it's very like TikTok is completely different people don't understand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic-source LTV benchmarking (§9 GAP — no written retention/churn strategy). Palm's whale-hunting dashboard (§9) tracks per-fan spend but does not appear to attribute fans to acquisition channel. This LTV signal is highly actionable for Palm's channel prioritisation decisions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add acquisition-channel tagging to Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so LTV can be broken down by source (IG reel, Reddit, etc.). Even before launching Reddit, build the measurement infra so that if/when Reddit is tested, LTV comparison data is captured from day one. Use the $8–$35 Reddit LTV benchmark as the hurdle rate when evaluating Reddit's cost-per-acquisition against account farming costs.

new_findings.title: Diversify traffic across platforms proactively to hedge against IG ban waves

variants.claim: Agencies relying exclusively on Instagram for traffic are highly exposed to ban waves; diversifying to other platforms (non-IG) immediately before or during a ban wave protects revenue and can generate significant incremental net profit within weeks.
variants.what_they_do: When IG bans hit Renato's clients hard, he prescribed a deliberate step-back from Instagram and shifted effort to unspecified 'other platforms' (intentionally not named — paid coaching content). Client Ricardo grew accounts on new platforms from scratch organically (no paid ads), saw minimal traction in week one, then hit 20K extra net profit in the following 10 days using 3–4 accounts totaling ~33K followers. Renato describes the method as 'full AI marketing' targeting USA audiences. The tactic is explicitly framed as platform-risk mitigation, not just growth.

variants.claim: Reliable revenue requires combining several traffic sources rather than relying on one, with each platform graded on traffic quality, not raw views.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends Instagram (primary, highest-quality subs), Threads, and X together, with TikTok optional; X drives high volume but lower-quality subs; explicitly moves away from pure rage-bait because '28.5M views' does not equal money — quality and niche of traffic matter for conversion.

variants.claim: No single platform is the permanent best traffic source — TikTok and Instagram Reels alternate in effectiveness over weeks, while Reddit bot services offer more stable, updatable automation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitors which platform is currently converting and shifts effort accordingly (e.g., moved from TikTok to IG Reels during a two-month TikTok slump, then back). Reddit bot/automation services are treated as a stable baseline because they can be updated when Reddit changes its algorithm or removes bots. Maintains awareness of Reddit API/bot policy changes.

variants.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.

sources.quote: we took a step back from Instagram as Instagram is banning way too many accounts right now
half of his Instagram accounts got banned. Half of your Instagram accounts get banned, you cut your pretty much revenue in half.
he got maybe 100, 200, 300 followers, not too much, but then in the remaining 10 days, when the accounts got a bit of traction, he's right now pretty much made 20K extra
you need to be using a combination of all different traffic sources
it doesn't always mean views equals money

there is no best it always varies Tick Tock for the last two weeks it is working but the last two months before that it was very very like it just genuinely it was just bad so Instagram reels were better so it always changes
things that work really really well at least for us that we found are like bot Services they don't really mess up and it's pretty easy to then update these bot Services as well
start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: §5 GAP — Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented. Palm uses IG and TikTok (via Publer/Telegram) but has no written multi-platform diversification policy or contingency plan for IG ban waves. §4 notes that real-creator warmup/cadence is not formalized. The IG ban risk is live and present for Palm's real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately document a platform-diversification contingency SOP: if any real creator loses >2 IG accounts in a 30-day window, trigger migration of effort to at least one backup platform. Identify which platforms (Reddit, TikTok, Threads, X, Snapchat) Palm can activate quickly given existing infrastructure. Add a 'backup platform' field to the Creators table in Airtable and assign one per active creator now, before a ban event forces a rushed response.

new_findings.topic: DM session structure and rapport phase
new_findings.title: Open every DM session with 5–20 message rapport phase before any sexual pivot

variants.claim: Every DM session — whether with a new or returning subscriber — should open with a non-sexual rapport phase to qualify the fan and create emotional investment before monetization begins.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers: 5–20 messages gathering personal info (job, hobbies) with light flirting, zero sexual content. Returning subscribers: brief small talk, ask about their day, deploy inside jokes if available. Only after this rapport block does the chatter pivot toward sexting. The report section is explicitly never scripted — it must feel genuine — but everything after it is.

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

sources.quote: First thing you want to do when a fan comes to the page is build report. This is five to 20 messages depending on the fan and the communication
The report part is never scripted because it has to feel genuine, but everything after that is scripted
Step One is General conversation building report... step two is transitioning to a more sexual conversation
conserving your energy you should never be throwing all of your energy away
that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal rapport SOP exists. This is a high-signal build-toward finding. The distinction between scripted vs. unscripted phases is particularly useful for designing chatter training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, encode a mandatory unscripted rapport phase at session open. Define minimum message count by subscriber type (new: ~10 msgs; returning: ~5 msgs). Train chatters that this phase is intentionally freeform — the goal is genuine connection data that feeds downstream personalization. Document inside-joke capture as a CRM field (Palm already has Fan Tracker §9 — add an 'inside jokes / personal details' field per fan).

new_findings.title: Track 5 profit layers, not just creator gross revenue

variants.claim: Creator gross revenue is the least useful number for judging agency health; founders must track five sequential layers down to owner profit per hour.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.

variants.claim: Agencies obsessing over gross revenue figures (e.g. $100K/mo) often keep only 10–40% as net profit, so every strategic decision should be benchmarked against what the operator actually takes home.
variants.what_they_do: Renato coaches clients to frame all goals as net-profit targets (e.g. 'I want to keep $50K/mo') rather than gross figures. He illustrates with a client doing ~$100K gross who keeps $65–70K via a salary-model structure vs. another doing $100K gross who keeps only $30–40K via a rev-share structure — same gross, very different outcomes. He explicitly tells clients to evaluate every agency decision (traffic platform, model structure, scaling direction) by the profit it will produce.

variants.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
variants.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.

sources.quote: level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour
profit is everything. Way too many people get confused in the game about fancy figures like a million a month
OnlyFans takes 20% if you're at a 100k gross revenue, you're left with 80K. His two salary models take less than 3K
Hit net profit targets. I want to keep 50k. I want to keep 100k. Every decision you make in your agency should be going towards this.
some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks creator gross via Inflow/FansMetric dashboards and computes agency net for invoicing (§7), but there is no documented gross-profit-per-creator, owner-profit, or owner-profit-per-hour metric. The invoicing system auto-computes splits and weekly PDFs, but stops at agency net — levels 3-5 of Dylan's waterfall are a GAP (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'profit waterfall' view in the admin portal: creator gross → agency net (already computed) → gross profit (subtract per-creator fulfillment costs: chat team allocation, editor time, VA time, software) → owner profit (subtract fixed overhead) → owner profit per founder hour. Surface this weekly alongside the existing invoice dashboard. This is a high-priority instrumentation task given Palm's current THIN baseline on profitability metrics.

new_findings.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can
you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall
you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

new_findings.topic: Commonality building as the core sales engine
new_findings.title: Three-tier commonality system to manufacture emotional bond

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Notably, the 'plausible reason for validation' logic is essentially applied sales psychology that Palm could encode as a training principle, not just a chatting script.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make Level 3 commonality building the centrepiece of Palm's in-house chatter training. Create a worked-example library organized by profession/hobby (e.g., 'if sub says he's a nurse, here are three Level 3 connections'). Pair this with the qualification CRM data so chatters can prep connections before re-engaging a returning sub.

new_findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

new_findings.title: Never pause acquisition — run it as a 24/7 autopilot system

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

variants.claim: Recruiting is the primary scaling lever for an OFM agency because creator quality and quantity directly determine revenue ceiling, yet most agencies treat it as an afterthought rather than a dedicated, always-on department.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs recruiting as a named department with dedicated effort and headcount, not delegated to the CEO's spare time. The principle is that the ideal creator (great look, great work ethic, fully cooperative) effectively makes the agency's systems and marketing effort secondary — and that five such creators could produce $200–300k/month. Recruiting never pauses regardless of current creator count.

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

sources.quote: the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running
Your recruiting shouldn't stop. Your recruiting shouldn't be left on the side. It's a full department in your agency that if you don't take it seriously, you're always going to be stuck.
the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and effectively pauses when attention shifts to delivery (§6). There is no documented funnel, VSL, or call-setting layer, and acquisition is not yet automated or delegated.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's current outreach method into an SOP, then hire/delegate outreach execution so acquisition runs independently of delivery. Add a funnel layer (lead-magnet video → VSL → booked call) to qualify higher-tier creators before the call. Treat acquisition as an always-on background process, not a sequential phase.

new_findings.title: Only sign creators you genuinely believe in and who demonstrate required work ethic

variants.claim: Signing creators indiscriminately — especially by pulling money from your own pocket to invest in unproven talent — leads to repeated disappointment; selective signing with reinvestment funded only from page revenue is more sustainable.
variants.what_they_do: He only signs models he believes he can help and who show the work ethic to succeed. He has stopped funding upfront investment from his own pocket after being burned repeatedly — all reinvestment now comes from the page's own revenue. He describes his current pitch as entirely numbers-based with no lifestyle promises, filtering for creators who respond to data over hype.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: I only sign models that I'm interested in working with and that I think I can help
I've gotten away from pulling out of my own pocket and putting money into somebody because I've been disappointed way too many times
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no systematic qualification criteria documented (§6 GAP). There is no written scorecard for what makes a creator signable vs. not, and no documented policy on whether Palm fronts any upfront costs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm creator qualification scorecard covering: existing audience size/engagement, content production consistency, responsiveness/work ethic signals, and revenue potential estimate. Document whether Palm ever funds upfront costs and under what conditions. Use a data-first pitch deck (projected revenue range, what drives each lever) rather than lifestyle/brand promises — this also filters for more serious creators.

new_findings.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced
you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

new_findings.topic: Threads as underrated early-mover traffic source
new_findings.title: Threads new-account first-post boost: exploit algorithmic push for fast early traffic

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

variants.claim: Creating a Threads account from an existing Instagram account gives an immediate follower boost that accelerates early growth.
variants.what_they_do: TDM connects each creator's existing IG account to Threads so that existing Instagram followers automatically carry over at account creation, providing an instant audience base rather than starting from zero.

variants.claim: High early posting frequency on Threads accelerates follower growth because the platform still incentivises new content volume.
variants.what_they_do: TDM posts 5–8 times per day per Threads account in the first weeks. Once the account hits 1,000 followers (or after a couple of weeks), they reduce cadence to 3–5 posts per day. They mix carousel posts (highest performers) with shorter comment/text posts as filler.

sources.quote: Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views
use an account that you already have on Instagram to make the threads account because this will give you a boost, a kickstart from all the followers that you have coming over
5 to eight posts per day per account. Now, after a couple of weeks or when you reach 1,000 followers, you can back off a little bit to three, maybe five posts a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting/distribution strategy is not documented for Threads (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm uses Publer for IG/TikTok scheduling but Threads is not mentioned. No Telegram-as-intermediate-funnel step is documented at Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Test Threads for AI personas immediately while the new-account boost window exists. Start with simple text-based engagement posts. For the AI persona line, use the Telegram intermediate step (persona Telegram → Fanvue) since it adds a warm pre-sell layer before the paid platform. Evaluate adding Threads to Publer scheduling (Phase 3 — §5).

new_findings.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
new_findings.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

new_findings.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
new_findings.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process
We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

new_findings.title: Match deal structure to creator tier, not one-size 50/50

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

sources.quote: you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K
my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets a single per-creator commission % at onboarding with no documented tier/retainer/upfront model (§7 THIN GAP — the #1 unwritten gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a tiered Palm pricing model (upfront setup fee for ad-acquired creators; retainer + upside split for established creators) instead of a flat commission, to protect margins and de-risk acquisition spend.

new_findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation
post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

new_findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

new_findings.topic: Fan segmentation — low vs high spenders
new_findings.title: Segment fans by max spend ($50 vs $120+) and use different opening tactics per tier

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that tracks spend data, but no documented segmented selling playbook or per-tier opener scripts. The data infrastructure exists; the chatter execution layer does not.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map these two tiers onto Palm's existing Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data. For the in-house chat team, write two distinct opener script banks keyed to max-spend threshold. The 'question game' opener is particularly low-friction and could be A/B tested quickly once chatting is brought in-house. The lapsed-whale personalized PPV ($150–200, notes-referenced caption) is a high-ROI re-engagement tactic worth standardizing.

new_findings.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

new_findings.title: Weekly cadence and standards prevent regression to chaos

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm coordinates via Telegram with no documented operating cadence, scorecards, or standards-enforcement rhythm (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Install a lightweight weekly cadence (priorities reset + one root-cause review) and a recognition/consequence standard across Palm's team and contractors.

new_findings.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

new_findings.title: IG mother/slave split: main account accumulates reach; disposable slave holds the OF link

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

variants.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
variants.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.

sources.quote: we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at
if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona warmup system (§3/§4) uses hardware-isolated GrapheneOS devices and aged accounts but does not document a mother/slave split specifically to protect link-in-bio from takedowns. Palm's real-creator accounts would not need this (ID-verified), but AI personas would.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (Amelia, Katie Rosie, Brielle — §3), implement the mother/slave IG architecture: main persona account never holds the OF/Beacons link directly; a linked secondary account absorbs takedown risk. Build a fast slave-account replacement SOP into the warmup playbook (§4).

new_findings.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
new_findings.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Motion reels — not static image slideshows or low-movement content — consistently achieve higher reach and should be the exclusive inspo source when building an AI creator's content library.
variants.what_they_do: When scanning Instagram for AI reel inspo, the operator filters specifically for 'motion reels' (active movement, not static poses) in the 100k–1M view range. Static content is excluded from the inspo pool regardless of view count.

sources.quote: Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides
always go for motion reels and always aiming for 100 to 1 million views reels

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

new_findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

variants.claim: Rather than committing to one chatting team immediately, rotating a new creator through multiple chat agencies on 48-hour trials surfaces the best-performing team for that creator.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter explicitly states: bounce the creator between chat teams with 48-hour rotations until finding the best one for her. This implies tracking per-team revenue performance over short windows and selecting the highest performer as the default team.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever
I would bounce the creator between chat teams with 48 hour rotations until I find the best one for her.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

new_findings.topic: X engagement farming via competitor reply hijacking
new_findings.title: Reply-Hijack Competitor X Posts With Relevant Matching Content to Steal Followers

variants.claim: Replying to competing creators' posts with a relevant matching photo of your model hijacks their engagement and drives profile visits and follows at zero cost.
variants.what_they_do: Identify 10–20 OF creator accounts in the same niche on X. When those accounts post (e.g., bikini beach pic), the burner account replies with a relevant matching photo of the managed creator and a natural caption (e.g., 'What about my bikini at the beach?'). Also retweet competitor posts to appear in front of their audience. Comment on any post going viral in the broader niche to farm free impressions. VA hired to execute this spam engagement loop throughout the day.

variants.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.

sources.quote: Reply to that with relevant things. What about my bikini at the beach? And then attach a photo of you at your model at a beach. What this is going to do is hijack their engagement.
You guys can hire a VA to do this. I have my VAs to do this. Just make sure your spam engagement.
Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no X presence (§5 GAP). Palm does no competitor engagement farming on any platform currently. The VA execution model aligns with Palm's contractor model (Amin for posting) — this would be a new VA task scope.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up X for a creator, hire or assign a VA specifically for X engagement tasks: daily competitor reply loop, retweet rotation, viral-post comment farming. Define a task card type in Airtable for 'X daily engagement' with a checklist. Budget ~1–2 hrs/day per creator for this role.

new_findings.title: Build a Telegram-sourced S4S network before signing creators to pre-load launch traffic

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month
before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented S4S or network-building playbook (§5 GAP). The system is entirely absent from Palm's current ops baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assign a part-time 'network getter' task (could be Josh or a VA) to join 5–10 OFM Telegram groups and begin logging trans-creator and agency contacts into an Airtable table (Name, Platform, Fan Count, Contact Method, Last Outreach, Deal Type). Even if Palm doesn't operate trans creators today, the mechanics of S4S and launch-day pre-scheduling are transferable to any niche and should be piloted on the next real-creator launch.

new_findings.title: Track the full outreach funnel and fix one stage at a time

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

variants.claim: Acquisition should be optimized on return-on-spend against creator lifetime value, not cost-per-lead, which lets you profitably pay far more for high-quality leads.
variants.what_they_do: Tracks average creator retention and revenue to derive LTV; with a 5k LTV avatar, a 500 dollar cost-per-lead beats a 100 dollar lead with low LTV. Once 100+ avatar leads are captured, builds 1% lookalike audiences to compound quality.

sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point
the focus metric always is ROI... cost per lead is a bad metric and doesn't really mean anything
have the balls to do that if you know that the average LTV is 5K, 10K, 20K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach funnel metrics (reply/booking/show/close) or follow-up cadence (§6 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Stand up an outbound tracker (reply, qualified, booked, showed, closed) and a multi-touch follow-up sequence, mirroring the data-driven discipline Palm already applies to content.

new_findings.topic: AI reel production — full synthetic content workflow
new_findings.title: Screenshot → GPT prompt → image gen → animation → CapCut caption: full AI reel pipeline

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

sources.quote: screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best
the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline is strong on inspo-driven ideation for real creators but does not include a fully synthetic reel production track. Palm does make AI social content for some real creators (§0 note) and has AI persona infrastructure (§3), but a codified screenshot→GPT-prompt→image-gen→animate→post workflow is not documented. This is AI-persona-relevant but the prompt-reverse-engineering technique is transferable to real-creator content ideation too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (§3, build-toward): document this 6-step synthetic reel workflow as a standing SOP. For real creators: evaluate whether the 'screenshot inspo frame → GPT detailed prompt → recreate visually' technique can improve brief quality in step 5 (Promote as filming idea) of the existing §1 pipeline. The 10-day test-then-scale cadence (post 10, pick winner, scale) is a low-cost experiment framework worth formalizing.

new_findings.title: Sustainable chat strategy: back off at spend thresholds to extend LTV

variants.claim: Guilt-tripping a fan past their comfort zone extracts a one-time spike but kills the relationship, whereas backing off at ~$1K converts that fan into $5–10K over a longer arc.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals financial strain, chatters are trained to ease pressure rather than escalate. The agency explicitly models the trade-off: short-term guilt-trip yields maybe 2–3x the current spend, then the fan churns permanently. Patience yields 5–10x over months. They contrast this with the 'milk-and-churn' approach (90-day lifecycle, maximize and discard) that the interviewer admits he ran previously.

variants.claim: Telling fans exactly what a locked video contains — rather than implying they'll 'finally see everything' — lets the fan self-qualify, reduces regret, and keeps them spending longer.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to describe PPV content as accurately and enticingly as possible without lying about what's actually shown. For non-nude creators especially, they never imply the fan is about to see explicit content they won't actually get. They still use persuasion and soft negotiation, but the content description itself is honest.

sources.quote: that $1,000 that you're going to get now could have been 3,000 or it could be 5 to 10,000 over a longer period of time
we tell them 'this is what you're going to see in this video' and I think that's a really important component where he can make his own decision on whether he's going to purchase it or not

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced (§8) — no internal SOP exists for fan spend pacing or LTV optimization. This is precisely the build-toward intelligence needed for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOP, codify a spend-threshold rule: once a fan signals financial stress, chatters shift to relationship-maintenance mode (check-ins, free previews, light asks) for a cooldown window before re-escalating PPV pushes. Define explicit LTV targets per fan tier rather than maximizing any single session.

new_findings.title: Manage creator expectations week-by-week or they invent their own

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

variants.claim: Managing a creator's expectations up front — covering communication channels, response SLAs, and realistic revenue ramps per month — is the single most important onboarding step.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines and communicates: which platform to message on, guaranteed response windows, and what revenue/subscriber outcomes are realistic in months 1, 2, and 3. They crowdsource realistic benchmarks from a community of 1,000+ operators to give creators honest projections rather than over-promising.

sources.quote: if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

managing expectations so when I say managing expectations that includes when can she message you on what platform what can she expect in the first week in the first month the second month and the third month

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented expectation-setting framework in onboarding (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bake a week-1/month-1/month-2 expectation script into the onboarding call to reduce early churn.

new_findings.title: Supervised shadow → monitored practice → independent KPI ramp for new chatters

variants.claim: New chatters should follow a structured three-phase ramp: shadow existing chatters, then practice under live supervision, then work independently under KPI monitoring — rather than going live solo immediately.
variants.what_they_do: Phase 1 (first ~week): new chatter shadows and observes existing chatters in real shifts. Phase 2: chatter handles 2–3 hours of live chat themselves while a supervisor watches in real time. Phase 3: chatter works independently while a supervisor continues to monitor shifts, gives feedback, and answers questions. KPIs and quality controls are only introduced once the chatter reaches a stable independent baseline.

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

sources.quote: let them watch other people, let them supervise other chatters for a couple of days for the first week. Then let them do their initial practice, let them chat for like two or three hours themselves with a supervisor watching.
give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal ramp-up process (§8 outsourced). This structured ramp is essential for Palm's in-house chat team plan and maps well to Palm's existing supervisor/manager role structure (§10 chat_manager Clerk role exists).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this three-phase ramp as the standard onboarding track for all in-house chatters. Define the Phase 2→3 promotion trigger (e.g., supervisor scores 3 consecutive satisfactory shifts). Assign the chat_manager Clerk role as the supervisor for Phase 1–2 monitoring. Codify KPI thresholds before Phase 3 begins.

new_findings.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
variants.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: I took everything from before 2022 and I put that in one folder and now I created a new folder and it is called of content 2023 and from there I have q1 2 3 and 4
I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§2) is THIN on OF vault/PPV organization. The social pipeline (§1) is mature for top-of-funnel assets but there is no documented system for tagging, retrieving, or reselling vault content. This is a direct gap in the OF vault layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a vault taxonomy standard for Palm-managed creators: Year → Month → content-type label (e.g. '2024-03-booty', '2024-03-face'). Add a vault-labeling step to the existing editor workflow so every uploaded asset is tagged before the task is closed. Build a lightweight 'resell menu' Airtable view that surfaces labeled vault assets by creator for chatter/PPV use.

new_findings.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
