use fan notes to store this information

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

new_findings.title: Separate 'network getter' and 'queue manager' roles to operationalize promotion at scale

variants.claim: A $100K/month promo machine requires two distinct ops roles: one that expands the contact network daily and one that schedules, tracks, and enforces timing on every active promotion deal.
variants.what_they_do: Network getters are in Telegram groups every day adding new creator and agency contacts. Queue managers hold the schedule of every live S4S and GG deal, confirm partners post on time, create unique tracking links per promo, and run profit analysis to identify high-ROI partners for reinvestment. A separate media buying team handles paid IG meme-page buys. Reddit/X contractors distribute content as an additional traffic stream. All roles are concurrent and ongoing, not launch-only.

sources.quote: your network getters are reaching out to new leads and creators every day expanding the size of your network
your queue managers are constantly in combo with all of your network arranging shout out for shout out and GGs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Amin for manual posting and an external chat team but no equivalent network-getter or queue-manager role (§10 THIN). Traffic strategy is an explicit GAP (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to activate a distribution playbook, define these two roles explicitly in the org chart before hiring. In the short term, create a simple Airtable table ('Promo Queue') with fields: Partner, Deal Type, Cost, Scheduled Date, Confirmed Posted (Y/N), Tracking Link, Fans Delivered, Profit. This infrastructure costs nothing to build now and makes the queue-manager role immediately hirable when needed.

new_findings.topic: Virality pattern recognition as a learnable skill
new_findings.title: Train agency operators to diagnose WHY specific video moments drive views/comments

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) uses AI vision analysis to score viral potential (step 3: transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score). This is automated signal — but the human analytical skill of diagnosing WHY a specific moment works is not documented as a training output for Palm staff. Palm has the data layer; the interpretive training layer is a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'virality annotation' training protocol for Palm editors and content managers: when reviewing top-performing reels, require annotators to tag the exact frame/moment that drove the metric and the mechanism (hook, pattern interrupt, comment bait, etc.). Accumulate these as a labeled training set both for future AI scoring refinement and for human operator skill-building.

new_findings.topic: Agency visual map (Miro board) for bottleneck visibility
new_findings.title: Use a shared Miro board per agency with employees rated, revenue targets, and per-creator ratios

variants.claim: A single visual Miro board showing all employees with ratings, target revenue, timeline progress, current revenue, and per-creator ratios makes every bottleneck visible and creates a clear step-by-step operating orientation for the agency owner.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin builds and shares a Miro board with each student/client. The board contains: all team members with a performance rating next to each, target revenue goal, timeline to hit it, current revenue, and per-model revenue ratios. It is described as 'basic tracking' — not pulling granular IG KPIs — but the visual format makes bottlenecks immediately apparent. It is credited as the primary driver of Barry's jump from $80K to $100K/mo.

sources.quote: you built this entire like visual map of my agency that I've never seen in that way every bottleneck was just visible right there on the screen
we have every single one of your employees with a rating next to it the target revenue the progress to the timeline your current revenue your ratios for each model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an Airtable-based ops infrastructure (§0) and a whale-hunting/fan tracker dashboard (§9), but no documented single-view agency health board combining team ratings, revenue targets, timeline, and per-creator ratios. The SMM hub redesign (§11) is consolidating social ops but is not the same as an agency-level performance map.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm Agency Dashboard (Miro or equivalent) per managed creator that consolidates: team members and performance ratings, current vs. target revenue, timeline milestones, and per-creator revenue contribution ratio. This is low-tech but high-leverage — consider this the 'operator view' layer sitting above the existing Airtable data. Could be a new view within the Next.js admin portal.

new_findings.title: Commission creator-filmed ad creatives from agency-written scripts, not boosted organic posts

variants.claim: Boosting existing organic posts performs poorly as paid traffic; scripted video ads designed specifically for conversion (with hard CTAs) significantly outperform.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 distinct video ad scripts per model designed for paid promotion — not repurposed from organic content. Scripts follow a narrative/scenario structure (e.g. boyfriend-behind-camera POV, creator implied to be on OF). Each includes a hard CTA so viewers understand she has an OF page. Creator films all scripts as a batch. Agency tests all creatives at $40/day for 7 days, identifies winner(s), requests variation batch of the winner format, and continues iterating. Distinction from organic content pipeline: purpose, format, and CTA are all different.

sources.quote: I'm talking about actual creatives made specifically for this
we'll give five to 10 scripts for video ads per model... we'll run a testing phase for these ad creatives

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a strong data-driven organic content pipeline (§1) but no equivalent system for paid ad creatives — these are a distinct content type (neither 'social top-of-funnel' nor 'OF vault/PPV' in Palm's current taxonomy; they're a third type: paid acquisition creatives). §2 notes Palm is thin on OF vault content; this is a fourth content category Palm currently has no system for.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define 'paid ad creative' as a formal content type in Palm's content taxonomy alongside organic social and OF vault. Build a script bank template (scenario types, CTA formulas). Add an 'ad creative' task type in the Airtable content ops table (`applLIT2t83plMqNx`) so creators can be assigned scripted ad filming tasks distinct from organic reel tasks. This is the content-production prerequisite for the Meta ads traffic channel.

new_findings.topic: X content rules — tease vs. reveal
new_findings.title: No Full Nudity on X — Use Tease-Only Content to Pull Higher-Spending Fans

variants.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
variants.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'

sources.quote: Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault content is a documented GAP (§2 — thin). This finding reinforces a content tiering principle: social = tease, OF = explicit. Palm's social pipeline (§1) produces top-of-funnel content but has no explicit rule documented about nudity levels per platform. This is a rule Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-tier rule to Palm's SOP: X/IG/TikTok = tease only (no nudity, professional quality). OF vault = exclusive explicit. Document this as a creator briefing item in onboarding and in the editor task spec so editors never post full nudity to social channels.

new_findings.topic: Agency commission structure and cost stack
new_findings.title: 35–50% creator split with chatting cost (15–20%) and ad reinvestment (5–10%) baked in

variants.claim: A sustainable agency deal charges creators 35–50% gross, with ~15–20% allocated to chatting, 5–10% reinvested into paid traffic, and the remainder as agency margin.
variants.what_they_do: Agency takes 35–50% of creator revenue depending on workload. Chatting costs the agency 15–20% of that (paid to the chat company, which in his case is his own). Ad spend is a committed 5–10% reinvestment every month — not optional, not discretionary. Higher percentage deals correspond to heavier operational lift. Year-long contracts with a small exit fee, but models are let go without a fight if they want to leave.

sources.quote: we go anywhere from 35 to 50%. Sometimes we'll go a little higher depending on like how much work it is
the chatting component costs anywhere between 15 to 20%... then I'll commit a certain portion of the budget, anywhere between like 5 to 10% depending on the month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has commission % set at onboarding but no documented split rationale or reinvestment commitment (§7 GAP). The cost-stack breakdown (chatting % + ad reinvestment % + margin %) is exactly the modeling Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Palm pricing model that explicitly stacks: (1) chatting cost % (currently outsourced — know your actual rate), (2) committed monthly ad/traffic reinvestment %, (3) operational overhead %, (4) margin floor. Use this to set defensible creator commission tiers rather than ad-hoc per-creator negotiation. Document and show this math to creators during signing — it builds trust and justifies the split.

new_findings.topic: Agency build order / working backwards from the back end
new_findings.title: Build and scale chatting operations first, then add marketing — not the reverse

variants.claim: Agencies that brand and sell marketing first without a proven back end destroy creator pages; the correct build order is chatting infrastructure → proven traffic method → then branding/scale.
variants.what_they_do: He spent years perfecting and scaling the chatting operation before attempting full-service management. Only after he had a chat system that could fire up staff on demand for any traffic volume did he add a marketing component he knew worked at scale. He explicitly says he'd do it the same way again and criticizes agencies that invert this — branding first, outsourcing chatting to the cheapest provider, and delivering substandard back-end results.

sources.quote: I'm very glad that I worked backwards because I figured out the back end first and I figured out how to scale it
a ton of agencies out there they sort of work backwards, they brand first, and they figure out more or less how to do all the bells and whistles

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's build order has been roughly the inverse: strong content/social pipeline (§1 mature), outsourced chatting (§8), thin pricing/ops. This is a structural observation worth discussing at the leadership level — Palm's chatting dependency on a third party is the single largest revenue risk.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this framework to prioritize Palm's in-house chat roadmap. The argument is not just about margin — it's about control of the revenue engine. Accelerate the AI chatting project and in-house chatter hiring timeline. In parallel, ensure the current external chat team has documented SLAs and performance benchmarks so Palm isn't flying blind on the most critical revenue driver.

new_findings.title: Concrete performance guarantee reduces creator risk

variants.claim: A concrete, numeric performance guarantee (specific result in a specific window or you work for free) reduces perceived risk and differentiates from scam-wary creators' defaults.
variants.what_they_do: Uses guarantees like '2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free' or 'clear buying activity in chats within 14 days or we fund your next shoot up to 1,000 dollars'; pairs the offer with one main angle (speed/risk/ease).

sources.quote: if you don't get at least 2,000 paying subs in 30 days, we work for free until you do
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or risk-reversal offer (§7 THIN); creator risk concerns are the #1 issue in Palm's own research yet unaddressed in writing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written risk-reversal/guarantee tied to a measurable early win, addressing the creator-trust gap surfaced in Palm's own research.

new_findings.title: Productized fulfillment makes the creator the only variable

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is productized (§1 MATURE) but OF vault/PPV and chat delivery are not standardized (§2 THIN, §8 OUTSOURCED); no single end-to-end fulfillment machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's productized social pipeline into a full standardized fulfillment system (page setup, welcome message, funnel, chat) so new creators plug into one machine and revenue isn't concentrated in a few creators.

new_findings.title: Build content around the creator's niche and her own strengths

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is data-driven and mature for ideation (§1), but niche/positioning and creator-strength fit are not explicit fields in the flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a niche + creator-strengths step feeding the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas are filtered for fit, not just virality.

new_findings.title: Use weekly invoicing explicitly as a trust signal, not just a payment mechanism

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

sources.quote: always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already auto-generates weekly invoices (§7 — multi-account PDF via Puppeteer, Dropbox, Resend). However, Palm's invoicing system is described as an ops tool, not a trust/relationship tool. There is no documented practice of *explaining* the invoicing schedule during onboarding or positioning it as a transparency signal to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an 'invoicing walkthrough' step to the onboarding call checklist: show the creator the invoice format, confirm the payment cadence, and explicitly frame it as mutual accountability. This costs nothing — Palm's invoice infrastructure already exists — but closes the relationship-framing gap.

new_findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator a hobby-based niche to give the algorithm a clear target audience

variants.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
variants.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.

sources.quote: if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on data-driven ideation from viral inspo reels but does not document a niche-assignment step per creator. The system scrapes what is already viral broadly; it does not codify the step of choosing one thematic niche per creator to anchor the algorithm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'niche selection' step to Palm's creator onboarding and content pipeline (§1, step 5 or pre-step). For each creator, define one or two content niches based on their genuine interests. Feed that niche as a filter into the Apify scrape / Inspo Sources targeting so viral reference reels are pulled from that niche's top performers, not just broadly popular content.

new_findings.title: Position the agency offer around creator outcomes (time saved, revenue growth), not the 50% split

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or offer framing (§7 GAP). Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding with no stated rationale or tiering. This is Palm's #1 identified gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's standard offer narrative: lead with the two pain points (fan growth, time), show a concrete growth trajectory from an existing client, and frame the 50% split as a performance-aligned partnership rather than a fee. Consider creating a one-page 'offer doc' with revenue-growth evidence that can be shared during acquisition outreach.

new_findings.title: Match viral inspo accounts to creator's look-type before briefing

variants.claim: Sending creators viral content from models who look like them — same hair color, body type, vibe — dramatically increases the likelihood the creator will replicate it well and that the content will resonate with the right audience.
variants.what_they_do: Agency manually finds a model whose look closely matches the new creator (e.g., 'blonde cheerleader type'), identifies that model's currently-viral videos, and sends them as direct filming references. Creator replicates the format/dance/hook on IG and TikTok.

sources.quote: try and find her ideas for Content that are similar to her looking so if she's a blond kind of cheerleader type model then fantastic find another model who looks like her and is currently going viral

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo from a defined list of accounts and uses AI vision to score virality, but the step of matching inspo sources to a specific creator's look-type is not documented. Inspo may be pooled generically across creators rather than look-matched. This is a refinement opportunity inside the existing pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'look profile' field to the Creators table (hair color, body type, vibe tags). When a new creator onboards, seed their Inspo Sources list with accounts that match their look profile rather than only genre/category. The existing scrape + AI-score pipeline then handles the rest.

new_findings.title: Activate Twitter/X early as a high-virality explicit-content funnel

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

sources.quote: on Twitter you can get away with more not safeer workor content so AKA showing everything and the chance of that going viral is a lot higher and Twitter's full of that right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution stack is documented around IG/TikTok via Publer and Amin (§5), with Reddit mentioned as a gap but no Twitter/X SOP. Twitter is absent from the documented pipeline. This is a notable traffic gap (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add Twitter/X as a standard platform in the social setup checklist for new real-creator onboarding. Build a separate posting cadence for Twitter that allows more explicit previews (within ToS). This is likely a quick win — low setup cost, distinct audience from IG/TikTok, and higher conversion intent.

new_findings.title: Sort subscriber list by spend tier immediately upon account access

variants.claim: The first action inside a newly-accessed OF account should be sorting existing subscribers by spend to identify whales and avoid wasting chatter time on low-value fans.
variants.what_they_do: Upon first login to the creator's OF account, agency immediately sorts subscribers by total spend, identifying high-value fans to prioritize in DMs and deprioritizing or ignoring low-spend/no-spend subscribers.

sources.quote: organizing to subscribers seeing who's spent more and who's not organizing them so you don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) for ongoing fan intelligence, but there is no documented step to perform an initial subscriber spend-sort at the point of account onboarding. The whale-hunting system appears to be an ongoing operational tool rather than an onboarding trigger. Adding this as an explicit Day-1 onboarding action would seed the whale-hunting system immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'subscriber spend sort + whale identification' as a mandatory Day-1 step in the creator onboarding checklist. Output should be a list of top-10 spenders imported directly into the Whale Hunting / Fan Tracker tables so the external chat team has prioritized targets from day one of management.

new_findings.title: Keep OF link off satellite accounts — funnel to intermediate IG pages only

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

sources.quote: in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current documented setup (§5) routes traffic through a single creator IG → link-in-bio → OF. The satellite layer with link-segregation is not present. Palm's AI persona accounts do use Beacons link-in-bio (§3), which may face similar flagging risk if OF is referenced. This architecture principle — isolate the OF link to as few accounts as possible — is directly applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit current Palm creator IG setups: does every account that references OF carry the link? If so, consider a two-tier architecture where traffic-generation accounts funnel to a single 'conversion' account that holds the OF link. This reduces surface area for platform enforcement actions across the entire portfolio.

new_findings.title: Train chatters to exceed founder-level performance rather than chatting yourself

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to an external team today (§8) — this is already structurally aligned with Ellis's recommendation. The build-toward for Palm is in-house chat team training and AI chatting (§8 roadmap). Ellis's training-focus framing is directly applicable to designing the in-house chatter onboarding and quality-ramp process.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds toward an in-house chat team (§8 roadmap), design the programme around a 'train chatters to beat founder level' benchmark rather than a floor-level compliance model. Document what a 10/10 chat interaction looks like, build that into chatter SOPs and QA scoring, and assign a chat manager whose sole job is raising chatter skill — not just monitoring volume.

new_findings.title: Invoice weekly rather than monthly to reduce exposure to creator non-payment

variants.claim: Agencies worried about non-payment should invoice weekly rather than monthly, reducing the maximum amount at risk at any given time.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn mentions weekly invoicing as a practical risk-mitigation option for agencies that are anxious about creators running with earnings. He frames it as a simple operational adjustment rather than a trust-control mechanism, keeping the relationship dynamic healthy while capping downside.

sources.quote: if you like the source go join the Discord — I'm going to invoice them weekly

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already invoices weekly (§7) — this is already our practice. Palm is ahead here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: No change needed — Palm's weekly invoicing cadence already implements this best practice. Document this explicitly in creator onboarding materials as a feature ('you get clear weekly accounting') rather than leaving it implicit.

new_findings.title: 20% real / 80% AI mix to keep trust and volume

variants.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
variants.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.

sources.quote: 20% real content kept people trusting it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature data-driven social pipeline (§1) but no codified real-vs-AI blend ratio for a hybrid creator. This gives a concrete starting ratio.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default ~20-30% real / 70-80% AI blend for hybrid creators, front-loading real content during launch; codify the ratio in the SMM content spec.

new_findings.title: Bans driven by unbranded sexual content, not IP/proxies

variants.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
variants.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.

sources.quote: 90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is hardware-isolated with proxies/device separation (§3/§4); for REAL creators Palm has no documented IG ban-avoidance posture (§4 gap). This reframes the real-creator risk as content-led.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real-creator IG, prioritize branded non-explicit content and normal-user behavior over technical spoofing; document a real-creator ban-avoidance SOP.

new_findings.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.

new_findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

new_findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

consensus_risers.title: Free-trial subs, monetize later via PPV — not on the sub price

variants.claim: Operators run free or near-free subscriptions to maximize entries, then make essentially all revenue from in-DM PPV and tips rather than the subscription fee.
variants.what_they_do: Run a 7-day free trial (or $3.99 floor sub) with scarcity framing ('limited spots / offers left'); the sub itself is treated as a bonus while the real money comes from getting fans inside and selling price-locked PPV scripts, tips, wall content and a recurring 'girlfriend experience' (e.g. $500/week VIP).

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: I strongly, strongly stress that you run a free trial. You don't charge for subs
the girlfriend experience
don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/sub model (§7 THIN); chatting/PPV that this depends on is outsourced (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the low-friction-entry + monetize-in-DM model and a VIP/girlfriend-experience recurring tier as inputs to Palm's pricing model (§7) and in-house/AI chat roadmap (§8).

consensus_risers.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
consensus_risers.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure
if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale
if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

consensus_risers.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

variants.claim: Most OFM agencies confuse 'brand' with 'niche,' but a real creator brand answers three questions: who is your audience, what is the creator's unique identity, and what story/fantasy are you selling.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant uses a three-question brand framework — (1) specific audience demographic beyond 'men who like porn', (2) unique creator identity that inspires devotion, (3) specific fantasy/story and emotional levers to pull — and claims this framework has taken creators from zero to top 0.1% earning hundreds of thousands per month. He cites Camila and Bonnie Blue as examples of brand-driven success.

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy
most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
make it that her persona
you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) decides what to film from viral data but is not framed around per-creator niche/fantasy positioning; positioning is implicit, not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

consensus_risers.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
consensus_risers.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster
use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

consensus_risers.topic: Fan list segmentation for mass messages
consensus_risers.title: Segment subscriber list into 5+ tiers; send different mass messages per tier daily

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

sources.quote: We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody
number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no documented internal segmentation strategy. Palm's Fan Tracker / whale-hunting system (§9) collects fan spend data, which is the raw material for this segmentation — but there's no documented bridge from that data to mass message targeting. This is an actionable gap even before building in-house chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As an immediate step: share Palm's whale-hunting and Fan Tracker data with the outsourced chat team and request they implement list segmentation (minimum: new, active buyer, big spender, no-spender). As a build-toward step for in-house: define the 5 segment definitions in Airtable against fan spend fields and automate segment tagging so chatters can filter in CreatorHero or equivalent. For the AI chatting roadmap, list segmentation is the primary personalization input — design the data model to support it from day one.

consensus_risers.topic: Inbound creator acquisition via personal brand
consensus_risers.title: Build personal brand (YouTube + IG) so top creators DM you first

variants.claim: High-quality creators with good work ethic come inbound to agencies that have visible authority content (YouTube, IG reels), eliminating the need to chase them.
variants.what_they_do: Oliver posts OFM-focused reels on a dedicated IG account (~10K followers) and maintains a YouTube channel since 2023; creators DM him directly saying they're looking for an agency. He signs them on the first call. He explicitly states creators who DM you first already see you as an authority, while creators you have to chase tend to have poor work ethic.

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

sources.quote: this girl called DM'd me on my OFM Oliver Instagram where I post a bunch of reels
the creators worth taking on…they usually come and find you. You don't try to find them.
your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6 — THIN). No documented inbound content flywheel. This is the opposite motion: competitor earns inbound by investing in public-facing authority content, Palm currently spends effort chasing creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public-facing OFM authority presence for Palm — even a small dedicated IG account posting agency results/tips reels. This converts acquisition from outbound grind to inbound pull, and pre-qualifies creators (they arrive already trusting Palm). Start with one reel/week; track how many creator DMs it generates over 90 days.

consensus_risers.topic: Creator brand building as a distinct discipline from niche selection
consensus_risers.title: Build distinct creator brands (audience + identity + story) — not just niches

variants.claim: Most OFM agencies confuse 'brand' with 'niche,' but a real creator brand answers three questions: who is your audience, what is the creator's unique identity, and what story/fantasy are you selling.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant uses a three-question brand framework — (1) specific audience demographic beyond 'men who like porn', (2) unique creator identity that inspires devotion, (3) specific fantasy/story and emotional levers to pull — and claims this framework has taken creators from zero to top 0.1% earning hundreds of thousands per month. He cites Camila and Bonnie Blue as examples of brand-driven success.

variants.claim: Posting high volume of content (10 TikToks/day, 10 IG Reels/day) plateaus revenue at ~$10K/mo; defining who the creator actually is — her vibe, niche, audience, and what makes her different — is what breaks past that ceiling.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin forced Barry to stop posting entirely and answer 'who is this model?' before resuming. They built a persona/brand sheet for each creator covering identity, vibe, niche, and differentiator. Content strategy only resumes once that identity is locked. They report LTV increases of 20–30% and improved CVR after implementing this.

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

sources.quote: most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
Stop posting for a second and tell me who the model actually is
figuring out her identity such as her vibe her audience her niche and then yeah just what makes her different
once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator brand framework (§2 GAP and §1 partial). The content pipeline (§1) is strong on viral ideation but does not appear to systematically define creator identity, target audience persona, or narrative/fantasy positioning before deciding what content to film. This is a meaningful strategic gap in how Palm directs creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a structured brand-definition step upstream of the content pipeline (§1). Before a creator enters the inspo/filming cycle, document: (1) their specific target audience segment, (2) their unique identity differentiators vs. comparable creators, (3) the core fantasy/emotional narrative they sell. Store this in the Creators table in Airtable and use it as a filter when promoting inspo reels to creators in Step 5 of the pipeline.

consensus_risers.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

variants.claim: Being fully transparent with models about where their money goes, admitting when you're failing and why, and framing everything around their long-term income sustainability earns far more loyalty than over-promising.
variants.what_they_do: He tells creators explicitly where agency fees go and how ad spend is allocated. When metrics underperform, he proactively explains why rather than going silent. When a creator wants to leave, he releases them without conflict and often admits his own faults. He reports that 8 out of 10 creators who leave return. He pitches creators with numbers and technical detail — no yachts, no content houses, no collab promises.

variants.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying
being fully transparent is very important to me. If I'm failing at something, I will tell them I'm failing at this thing. Now I'll tell them why I'm failing at it
if you want to leave and go somewhere else, I'm not going to fight you... eight out of 10 times they'll revisit
a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

consensus_risers.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so
you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

consensus_risers.title: Give high spenders 1:1 personalized treatment, never mass messages

variants.claim: Retain top spenders by excluding them from mass blasts and giving escalating personalized touches as they cross spend thresholds.
variants.what_they_do: Tier fans by spend on a smart list; anyone over a threshold (e.g. $1,500) gets zero mass messages/PPVs, only personalized 1:1. Touches escalate: >$500 first night = personalized voice note (real or ElevenLabs AI); always call them by their saved nickname; >$2,500/mo = a short safe-for-work selfie video saying their name; occasional physical-prize raffles. One client made $56,500 in 30 days from her top 5 fans.

variants.claim: The highest-spending OnlyFans fans share three overlapping psychological drivers: loneliness/isolation, attraction to someone perceived as out of their league, and dopamine from the gamified PPV unlock mechanic.
variants.what_they_do: Based on running thousands of concurrent fan conversations, he identifies the primary demographic as blue-collar or mid-income men (truck drivers, ex-military, some tech/finance) aged 25–40, with disposable income but limited social and romantic access. He maps three compounding psychological levers: (1) loneliness solved by parasocial intimacy, (2) willingness to spend freely on someone perceived as unattainable, (3) gambling-like dopamine from the grayed-out PPV unlock. He uses this model to train chatters on sequencing: build the parasocial bond, anchor perceived unattainability, then introduce the PPV unlock at the peak of anticipation.

sources.quote: for big spending fans. You don't send them mass messages or mass pay-per-views... you want to give them that one-on-one personal interaction.
Anybody over $500 in their first night should get personalized voice notes... we can use AI voice notes... 11 Labs.
If the fan has spent more than $2.5,000 in one month, record a short personalized safer work selfie video saying his name.
largely these people are relatively lonely, right? And so this is the quickest solution. On top of that, they're speaking to someone that's hypothetically completely out of their league
the third component is that gamification aspect of it... the video that you send when you lock it for a specific price is sort of grayed out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale tooling (Fan Tracker, going-cold detection, smart-list-style data) but no written whale-nurture playbook (§9 PARTIAL/GAP); chatting is outsourced (§8), so the tactics are build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Turn whale data into an operating playbook: spend-threshold tiers, exclude-from-mass rule, nickname use, voice-note/selfie-video escalations (AI voice where applicable), raffles — build-toward for in-house/AI chat.

consensus_risers.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

consensus_risers.title: Offer content bundles to increase average order value over single-item PPV

variants.claim: Packaging multiple pieces of content together at a 'special price' increases perceived value and raises average transaction size.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters pitch bundles during PPV conversations: e.g., 'get three exclusive pics plus a surprise video for a special price.' The bundle is presented as a deal, not a push, and is integrated naturally into the flow of conversation rather than sent as a standalone promo.

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: offer bundles and packages people are more likely to buy if they feel like they're getting a better deal
you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or bundle structure (§7 GAP). Single-item PPV is presumably the default through the outsourced team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a tiered PPV menu: individual asset price, 3-item bundle at ~20% discount, and a monthly 'vault' bundle. Give the chat team (outsourced now, in-house later) explicit bundle scripts and price points so upselling is consistent across accounts.

consensus_risers.title: Deploy a chatter supervisor on Day 1 as a proactive asset, not a reactive fix

variants.claim: Adding a dedicated chat supervisor from day one — who reviews 50–60 conversations daily — catches revenue-destroying patterns weeks or months before they'd surface in KPI dashboards, preventing compounding subscriber loss.
variants.what_they_do: On day 1 they assign a 'Hulk supervisor' whose sole job is to pull and deep-analyze 50–60 chats per day. The supervisor checks: script adherence, objection-handling consistency, list management, correct content routing to fans. When within 7 days a pattern of subs dropping after first PPV was spotted, the supervisor flagged it to the backend manager, they reviewed PPV strategy for the week, adjusted price and script. Three chatters on 8-hour shifts give 24/7 coverage; all conversations answered within 70 seconds. Supervisor is added before problems appear — not as damage control after.

variants.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
variants.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.

sources.quote: we add the supervisor as an asset on day one…the supervisor pulls 50 to 60 chats a day and analyzes deeply
they catch the patterns of the chatters fast…within 7 days of the switch…the supervisor noticed that was like a chunk of subs that were dropping off after the first PPV
the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is currently outsourced (§8 — external team). No internal supervisor layer or QA system is documented. This is high-value build-toward intelligence for Palm's roadmap (in-house chat team ~1yr out + AI chatting project). The supervisor-as-day-1-asset framing is a direct structural recommendation for when Palm builds in-house.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, make the supervisor role a launch-day hire (not a later addition). Define the supervisor's daily output: minimum N chats reviewed, a weekly pattern report to ops, and a flag-to-fix SLA (e.g., pattern flagged → backend adjustment within 48 hours). For the AI chatting project, equivalent logic applies: build automated conversation QA from day one rather than retrofitting it. In the interim, ask the current outsourced team to provide weekly pattern reports (drop-off points, reply rate by message type) as a proxy.

consensus_risers.title: Founder should spend hours/day on the highest-leverage asset

variants.claim: Founders should reallocate large blocks of daily time to the single highest-leverage activity (brand/distribution) and treat inability to do so as a sign the business is structured wrong.
variants.what_they_do: Argues for 4+ hours/day on personal brand and says if you don't have that time, the business is set up wrong and you're stuck firefighting; pushes decentralizing operations so the founder works on leverage, not in the hamster wheel.

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: you need to be spending 4 hours a day on this. Like minimum
if you haven't got 4 hours... a day to put into your personal brand, you're doing something wrong with your business
take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10): Josh carries CEO + acquisition + strategy with no org chart or delegation plan; tech/ops concentrated on Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit founder time and protect daily blocks for the highest-leverage work (acquisition/brand), delegating firefighting — ties directly to the teammate-org initiative.

consensus_risers.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.
It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

consensus_risers.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

variants.claim: Deliberately overhire chatters and keep them on standby so that a sudden influx of new creator signings never forces a desperate, low-quality hire.
variants.what_they_do: After going through a 'firing spree' from bad chatters who couldn't adapt to a new training system, the speaker now intentionally maintains excess chatter capacity — paying people to be available rather than scrambling to hire when a new account comes online.
